comment_text.sn22.3:1.2: Mahākaccāna was one of the great disciples, whose teachings specially emphasized the incisive analysis of consciousness through the lens of the six senses. He was said to be the most skilled at given detailed explanations of brief teachings (AN 1.197), a skill he displayed also at MN 133, MN 138, and AN 10.172. He settled to the southwest in Avanti, where he established the Dhamma in the region; see SN 35.132 and notes..
comment_text.sn22.3:1.3: Hāliddikāni was a learned lay student of Mahākaccāna in Avanti (SN 22.4, SN 35.130). | From this point, the discourse is quoted in full at Mnd 9:70.1 as a commentary on the verse. This underscores the fact that even within the Suttas themselves, there are many passages that can be considered commentaries on earlier discourses.
comment_text.sn22.3:2.1: This line works perfectly well at the literal level, the life of a homeless renunciate. But Mahākaccāna reveals a metaphorical dimension, which pivots on the fact that the suffix sārī (“migrate”) has the same root as saṁsāra (“transmigration”). | Oka is an old Vedic word for “home” (Rig Veda 5.76.4) for people, gods, or even cows (RV 6.41.1) with connotations of safety and refuge from which one might be driven out by a foe (RV 7.5.6).
comment_text.sn22.3:4.1: Note the use of dhātu (“element”) instead of khandha (“aggregate”). Also at SN 22.4, SN 22.45, SN 22.53–5.
comment_text.sn22.3:4.2: They wander from life to life, like “people entering and leaving a house, walking along the streets and paths, and sitting at the central square” (DN 2:96.1, MN 39:20.3). As in dependent origination, “consciousness” is key to transmigration as the link from one life to the next (SN 12.38–40).
comment_text.sn22.3:6.1: The commentary explains the difference between “bastion” (oka) and “abode” (niketa) as one of degree. A bastion is a place where one is established in comfort and safety and is thus tightly attached as to the aggregates. An abode is any old place one might stay, and are therefore lightly attached to, like the passing parade of sense experiences.
comment_text.sn22.3:6.2: Pali visāra seems unique (at DN 13:36.2 read visāda). Here it is clearly connected to sārī in the sense of “migrant”, as well as anusārī in the phrase rūpanimittānusāri (“follows after the features of that sight”, MN 138:10.2). I take it as an intensive form of “follow”, i.e. “chase”.
comment_text.sn22.3:14.3: Sahita (“on topic”) appears routinely as a quality of a good Dhamma talk (eg. AN 4.139:3.2, AN 8.16:1.3). It has the basic sense of “collected, bundled” (like firewood, DN 23:21.49), in which sense it appears in Sanskrit as saṁhita, the name of the “bundled” collection of Vedic scripture.

root_text.sn22.3:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ āyasmā mahākaccāno avantīsu viharati kuraraghare papāte pabbate.
root_text.sn22.3:1.3: Atha kho hāliddikāni gahapati yenāyasmā mahākaccāno tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā āyasmantaṁ mahākaccānaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho hāliddikāni gahapati āyasmantaṁ mahākaccānaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn22.3:3.1: Imassa nu kho, bhante, bhagavatā saṅkhittena bhāsitassa kathaṁ vitthārena attho daṭṭhabbo”ti?
root_text.sn22.3:5.2: Rūpadhātuyā kho, gahapati, yo chando yo rāgo yā nandī yā taṇhā ye upayupādānā cetaso adhiṭṭhānābhinivesānusayā te tathāgatassa pahīnā ucchinnamūlā tālāvatthukatā anabhāvaṅkatā āyatiṁ anuppādadhammā.
root_text.sn22.3:5.7: viññāṇadhātuyā kho, gahapati, yo chando yo rāgo yā nandī yā taṇhā ye upayupādānā cetaso adhiṭṭhānābhinivesānusayā te tathāgatassa pahīnā ucchinnamūlā tālāvatthukatā anabhāvaṅkatā āyatiṁ anuppādadhammā.
root_text.sn22.3:7.2: Rūpanimittaniketavisāravinibandhā kho, gahapati, tathāgatassa pahīnā ucchinnamūlā tālāvatthukatā anabhāvaṅkatā āyatiṁ anuppādadhammā.
root_text.sn22.3:7.8: dhammanimittaniketavisāravinibandhā kho, gahapati, tathāgatassa pahīnā ucchinnamūlā tālāvatthukatā anabhāvaṅkatā āyatiṁ anuppādadhammā.
root_text.sn22.3:8.2: Idha, gahapati, ekacco gihīhi saṁsaṭṭho viharati sahanandī sahasokī, sukhitesu sukhito, dukkhitesu dukkhito, uppannesu kiccakaraṇīyesu attanā tesu yogaṁ āpajjati.
root_text.sn22.3:8.3: Evaṁ kho, gahapati, gāme santhavajāto hoti.
root_text.sn22.3:9.1: Kathañca, gahapati, gāme na santhavajāto hoti?
root_text.sn22.3:9.2: Idha, gahapati, bhikkhu gihīhi asaṁsaṭṭho viharati na sahanandī na sahasokī na sukhitesu sukhito na dukkhitesu dukkhito, uppannesu kiccakaraṇīyesu na attanā tesu yogaṁ āpajjati.
root_text.sn22.3:9.3: Evaṁ kho, gahapati, gāme na santhavajāto hoti.
root_text.sn22.3:10.2: Idha, gahapati, ekacco kāmesu avigatarāgo hoti avigatacchando avigatapemo avigatapipāso avigatapariḷāho avigatataṇho.
root_text.sn22.3:10.3: Evaṁ kho, gahapati, kāmehi aritto hoti.
root_text.sn22.3:11.2: Idha, gahapati, ekacco kāmesu vigatarāgo hoti vigatacchando vigatapemo vigatapipāso vigatapariḷāho vigatataṇho.
root_text.sn22.3:11.3: Evaṁ kho, gahapati, kāmehi ritto hoti.
root_text.sn22.3:12.3: ‘evaṁrūpo siyaṁ anāgatamaddhānaṁ, evaṁvedano siyaṁ anāgatamaddhānaṁ, evaṁsañño siyaṁ anāgatamaddhānaṁ, evaṁsaṅkhāro siyaṁ anāgatamaddhānaṁ, evaṁviññāṇo siyaṁ anāgatamaddhānan’ti.
root_text.sn22.3:13.2: Idha, gahapati, ekaccassa na evaṁ hoti:
root_text.sn22.3:13.3: ‘evaṁrūpo siyaṁ anāgatamaddhānaṁ, evaṁvedano siyaṁ anāgatamaddhānaṁ, evaṁsañño siyaṁ anāgatamaddhānaṁ, evaṁsaṅkhāro siyaṁ anāgatamaddhānaṁ, evaṁviññāṇo siyaṁ anāgatamaddhānan’ti.
root_text.sn22.3:14.1: Kathañca, gahapati, kathaṁ viggayha janena kattā hoti?
root_text.sn22.3:14.2: Idha, gahapati, ekacco evarūpiṁ kathaṁ kattā hoti:
root_text.sn22.3:14.3: ‘na tvaṁ imaṁ dhammavinayaṁ ājānāsi; ahaṁ imaṁ dhammavinayaṁ ājānāmi. Kiṁ tvaṁ imaṁ dhammavinayaṁ ājānissasi? Micchāpaṭipanno tvamasi; ahamasmi sammāpaṭipanno. Pure vacanīyaṁ pacchā avaca; pacchā vacanīyaṁ pure avaca. Sahitaṁ me, asahitaṁ te. Adhiciṇṇaṁ te viparāvattaṁ. Āropito te vādo; cara vādappamokkhāya. Niggahitosi; nibbeṭhehi vā sace pahosī’ti.
root_text.sn22.3:14.4: Evaṁ kho, gahapati, kathaṁ viggayha janena kattā hoti.
root_text.sn22.3:15.1: Kathañca, gahapati, kathaṁ na viggayha janena kattā hoti?
root_text.sn22.3:15.2: Idha, gahapati, bhikkhu na evarūpiṁ kathaṁ kattā hoti:
root_text.sn22.3:15.3: ‘na tvaṁ imaṁ dhammavinayaṁ ājānāsi …pe… nibbeṭhehi vā sace pahosī’ti.
root_text.sn22.3:15.4: Evaṁ kho, gahapati, kathaṁ na viggayha janena kattā hoti.
root_text.sn22.3:16.1: Iti kho, gahapati, yaṁ taṁ vuttaṁ bhagavatā aṭṭhakavaggiye māgaṇḍiyapañhe:
root_text.sn22.3:18.1: Imassa kho, gahapati, bhagavatā saṅkhittena bhāsitassa evaṁ vitthārena attho daṭṭhabbo”ti.

translation_text.sn22.3:1.2: At one time Venerable Mahākaccāna was staying in the land of the Avantis near Kuraraghara on Steep Mountain.
translation_text.sn22.3:1.3: Then the householder Hāliddikāni went up to Venerable Mahākaccāna, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him,
translation_text.sn22.3:1.4: “Sir, this was said by the Buddha in the Chapter of the Eights, in ‘The Questions of Māgandiya’:
translation_text.sn22.3:2.1: ‘After leaving their bastion to migrate without abode,
translation_text.sn22.3:2.2: a sage doesn’t get close to anyone in town.
translation_text.sn22.3:2.3: Rid of sensual pleasures, not expecting,
translation_text.sn22.3:2.4: they wouldn’t get in arguments with people.’
translation_text.sn22.3:3.1: How should we see the detailed meaning of the Buddha’s brief statement?”
translation_text.sn22.3:4.1: “Householder, the form element is a shelter for consciousness.
translation_text.sn22.3:4.2: One whose consciousness is shackled by greed for the form element is called a migrant going from bastion to bastion.
translation_text.sn22.3:4.3: The feeling element is a shelter for consciousness.
translation_text.sn22.3:4.4: One whose consciousness is shackled to greed for the feeling element is called a migrant going from bastion to bastion.
translation_text.sn22.3:4.5: The perception element is a shelter for consciousness.
translation_text.sn22.3:4.6: One whose consciousness is shackled to greed for the perception element is called a migrant going from bastion to bastion.
translation_text.sn22.3:4.7: The choices element is a shelter for consciousness.
translation_text.sn22.3:4.8: One whose consciousness is shackled to greed for the choices element is called a migrant going from bastion to bastion.
translation_text.sn22.3:4.9: That’s how one is a migrant going from  bastion to bastion.
translation_text.sn22.3:5.1: And how is one a migrant with no bastion?
translation_text.sn22.3:5.2: The Realized One has given up any desire, greed, relishing, and craving for the form element; any attraction, grasping, mental fixation, insistence, and underlying tendencies. He has cut it off at the root, made it like a palm stump, obliterated it, so it’s unable to arise in the future.
translation_text.sn22.3:5.3: That’s why the Realized One is called a migrant with no bastion.
translation_text.sn22.3:5.4: The Realized One has given up any desire, greed, relishing, and craving for the feeling element …
translation_text.sn22.3:5.7: the consciousness element; any attraction, grasping, mental fixation, insistence, and underlying tendencies. He has cut it off at the root, made it like a palm stump, obliterated it, so it’s unable to arise in the future.
translation_text.sn22.3:5.8: That’s why the Realized One is called a migrant with no bastion.
translation_text.sn22.3:5.9: That’s how one is a migrant with no shelter.
translation_text.sn22.3:6.1: And how is one a migrant going from abode to abode?
translation_text.sn22.3:6.2: Shackled to chasing the abodes that are the features of sights, one is called a migrant going from abode to abode.
translation_text.sn22.3:6.3: Shackled to chasing the abodes that are the features of sounds …
translation_text.sn22.3:6.7: thoughts, one is called a migrant going from abode to abode.
translation_text.sn22.3:6.8: That’s how one is a migrant going from abode to abode.
translation_text.sn22.3:7.1: And how is one a migrant without abode?
translation_text.sn22.3:7.2: The Realized One has given up chasing the abodes that are the features of  sights. He has cut it off at the root, made it like a palm stump, obliterated it, so it’s unable to arise in the future.
translation_text.sn22.3:7.3: That’s why the Realized One is called a migrant without abode.
translation_text.sn22.3:7.4: The Realized One has given up chasing the abodes that are the features of sounds …
translation_text.sn22.3:7.8: ideas. He has cut it off at the root, made it like a palm stump, obliterated it, so it’s unable to arise in the future.
translation_text.sn22.3:7.9: That’s why the Realized One is called a migrant without abode.
translation_text.sn22.3:7.10: That’s how one is a migrant without abode.
translation_text.sn22.3:8.1: And how does one get close to people in town?
translation_text.sn22.3:8.2: It’s when someone mixes closely with laypeople, sharing their joys and sorrows—happy when they’re happy and sad when they’re sad—and getting themselves involved in their business.
translation_text.sn22.3:8.3: That’s how one gets close to people in town.
translation_text.sn22.3:9.1: And how does one not get close to people in town?
translation_text.sn22.3:9.2: It’s when a mendicant doesn’t mix closely with laypeople, not sharing their joys and sorrows—not happy when they’re happy or sad when they’re sad—and not getting themselves involved in their business.
translation_text.sn22.3:9.3: That’s how one doesn’t get close to people in town.
translation_text.sn22.3:10.1: And how is one not rid of sensual pleasures?
translation_text.sn22.3:10.2: It’s when someone isn’t rid of greed, desire, fondness, thirst, passion, and craving for sensual pleasures.
translation_text.sn22.3:10.3: That’s how one is not rid of sensual pleasures.
translation_text.sn22.3:11.1: And how is one rid of sensual pleasures?
translation_text.sn22.3:11.2: It’s when someone is rid of greed, desire, fondness, thirst, passion, and craving for sensual pleasures.
translation_text.sn22.3:11.3: That’s how one is rid of sensual pleasures.
translation_text.sn22.3:12.3: ‘In the future, may I be of such form, such feeling, such perception, such choices, and such consciousness!’
translation_text.sn22.3:13.1: And how is one not expecting?
translation_text.sn22.3:13.3: ‘In the future, may I be of such form, such feeling, such perception, such choices, and such consciousness!’
translation_text.sn22.3:13.4: That’s how one is not expecting.
translation_text.sn22.3:14.1: And how does one argue with people?
translation_text.sn22.3:14.2: It’s when someone takes part in this sort of discussion:
translation_text.sn22.3:14.3: ‘You don’t understand this teaching and training. I understand this teaching and training. What, you understand this teaching and training? You’re practicing wrong. I’m practicing right. You said last what you should have said first. You said first what you should have said last. I stay on topic, you don’t. What you’ve thought so much about has been disproved. Your doctrine is refuted. Go on, save your doctrine! You’re trapped; get yourself out of this—if you can!’
translation_text.sn22.3:14.4: That’s how one argues with people.
translation_text.sn22.3:15.1: And how does one not argue with people?
translation_text.sn22.3:15.2: It’s when a mendicant doesn’t take part in this sort of discussion:
translation_text.sn22.3:15.3: ‘You don’t understand this teaching and training … get yourself out of this—if you can!’
translation_text.sn22.3:15.4: That’s how one doesn’t argue with people.
translation_text.sn22.3:16.1: So, householder, that’s how to understand the detailed meaning of what the Buddha said in brief in the Chapter of the Eights, in ‘The Questions of Māgandiya’:
translation_text.sn22.3:17.1: ‘After leaving their bastion to migrate without abode,
translation_text.sn22.3:17.2: a sage doesn’t get close to anyone in town.
translation_text.sn22.3:17.3: Rid of sensual pleasures, not expecting,
translation_text.sn22.3:17.4: they wouldn’t get in arguments with people.’”

variant_text.sn22.3:5.2: nandī → nandi (bj, sya-all, km, pts1ed) | upayupādānā → upāyupādānā (sya-all, km, pts1ed) | anabhāvaṅkatā → anabhāvakatā (bj, pts1ed)

reference_text.sn22.3:4.1: cck17.10, csp1ed13.9, csp2ed13.9, ms13S3_38, pts-vp-pli3.10, sya17.13
